species: Red-tailed Hawk -- sustained territorial scream sequence
sound_type: Sustained territorial scream sequence: adult Red-tailed Hawk defending home range by issuing repeated kee-eeeee-arr calls at 3-8 second intervals, 5-20 repetitions, directed at territorial stimulus (intruder hawk, approaching predator, human). Sequence accompanied by postural display. Individual call: 2-4 seconds, 1-3 kHz primary, harmonics to 6 kHz. Propagation: 1 km+ in still air over open terrain. ACCIPITRIDAE RETURN. HOLLYWOOD MISAPPROPRIATION DOCUMENTED.
ecosystem: Open valley, agricultural slope, open ridge, riparian edge -- habitats with unobstructed sightlines and open acoustic propagation over flat terrain. PNW contexts: Willamette Valley, Puget Sound lowlands, Skagit Valley, Columbia Plateau agricultural areas, Cascades foothills open slopes. Perch use: fence posts, lone snags, utility poles, upper branches of isolated trees in open fields.
season: Resident (year-round). Peak territorial calling: February-June during breeding season. Territory defense year-round but most intense when pair-bonding, nest-building, and fledgling protection are active.
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Widespread and common throughout North America. Most abundant large hawk in PNW open habitats. Not threatened.
milestone: ACCIPITRIDAE RETURN. E=7. Sequence entry -- documents not a single call but the full territorial behavioral communication: call sequence (5-20 calls), inter-call interval (3-8 seconds), directional calling, postural display coordination, stimulus-escalation logic. HOLLYWOOD MISAPPROPRIATION: the RTH kee-eeeee-arr is the most culturally misrepresented wildlife call in North America -- dubbed over Bald Eagle images in virtually all film/TV productions. Engine documents the actual call in actual behavioral context.

coordinates.description: Puget Sound lowlands and Cascade foothills: the RTH's primary PNW acoustic territory zone. Willamette Valley (OR), Columbia Plateau (WA): major agricultural landscape territories. Skagit Valley (WA): high-density resident population. Olympic Peninsula forest margins: edge habitat territories.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Territorial scream: kee-eeeee-arr -- a hoarse, descending call with sharp high-frequency onset (~2-4 kHz), sustained tonal mid-element, descending raspy end. The most acoustically distinctive North American hawk call. Individual calls audible at 1+ km in still air over open terrain. Produced by the syrinx (avian vocal organ at trachea-bronchi junction) using single-channel output; the RTH does not exploit the dual-channel capability common in songbirds.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Syrinx-mediated vocalization: the syrinx sits at the junction of the trachea and bronchi, enabling birds to produce two independent acoustic channels (one per bronchus). The RTH primarily uses single-channel production for its territorial call. Call amplitude is proportional to body size (females, being larger in Buteonines, can produce louder calls). The descending frequency profile (onset high, end lower/raspy) is characteristic of most hawk alarm and territorial calls and carries well over open terrain.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Primary acoustic energy: 1-3 kHz. Harmonics: to 6 kHz. The mid-frequency range (1-3 kHz) coincides with the peak sensitivity of human hearing and the optimal propagation band over open flat terrain. No infrasonic components. Call is fully within human hearing range and perceived as loud and clear at distances up to 500m in field conditions.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7: (1) call amplitude: the RTH territorial scream is among the loudest hawk calls in the PNW, carrying 1 km+ over open terrain; (2) sequence structure: the sustained territorial sequence (5-20 calls) constitutes a behavioral communication event at E=7, not just a single E=6 call unit; (3) directional precision: the call is directed at a specific stimulus at close range, escalating with intruder persistence -- the energy of the sequence is purposeful; (4) open-valley propagation context: the call is optimized for maximum acoustic territory coverage; (5) ACCIPITRIDAE return context: E=7 from purposeful sustained precision.

nodes.title: Accipitridae Return -- Sequence Entry
nodes.description: Red-tailed Hawk returns the Accipitridae family to the engine at degree 319. First RTH appearance at earlier degree established the species. Degree 319 adds the sequence analysis: the full territorial call sequence as a behavioral communication event, not just the individual call unit. The sequence is the message; the call is the word.

nodes.title: Hollywood Call Misappropriation -- The Most Culturally Misrepresented Wildlife Sound
nodes.description: The RTH kee-eeeee-arr is dubbed over Bald Eagle images in virtually all film/TV productions. Reason: the Bald Eagle's actual call (thin, weak, high-pitched twittering) fails the cinematic majesty test. The RTH scream (1-3 kHz, hoarse, descending, loud) satisfies it. Result: millions of people know the RTH call attributed to other species. The engine documents the actual behavioral context: territorial sequence, open valley, directed stimulus. Not a cinematic symbol.

nodes.title: Territorial Sequence as Complete Communication Event
nodes.description: 5-20 calls at 3-8 second intervals, directed at territorial stimulus, escalating with intruder persistence. The sequence is not random repetition but a structured behavioral message: stay-away, I-am-here, this-territory-is-mine. Accompanies postural display. Escalation logic: silence (30-60 sec observation) -> call sequence (5-20 calls) -> aerial display if intruder persists. The sequence documents the full behavioral algorithm of territorial defense.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. PRECISION PAIRING: both reach E=7 through structured escalation motivated by specific stimulus (intruder for RTH; emotional content for Akre). Both resident. Both E=7 through purposeful precision.
First Accipitridae entry (E=5, thin descending kee-kee-kee). RTH (319) returns the family at E=7 with the full territorial scream sequence. Accipitridae E arc: 5 (221) -> 7 (297) -> 7 (319).

Consecutive degrees 319 and 320 are both E=7. Accipitridae (RTH, precision territorial) followed immediately by Corvidae (Raven, cognitive complexity). The two families enter consecutively: hawk precision vs. raven intelligence, both at E=7.
